title: Neem cold-process soap
subtitle: A deeply cleansing bar with 15% neem oil for oily and problem-prone skin
excerpt: Neem oil has a distinctive earthy, sulphurous scent that mellows considerably with a long cure. At 15% in this formula it delivers its skin-clearing properties without overpowering the bar, which is otherwise a classic olive and coconut base.
sourceNotes: Formulation based on standard cold-process soap-making references. Neem oil properties referenced from Neem Foundation technical notes and Journal of Ethnopharmacology.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~7 x 130 g bars after 4-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally and rerun through a lye calculator before making.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bars require a minimum 4-week cure. The neem scent softens and becomes far more pleasant after 6 to 8 weeks.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine lye solution and oils at 40 to 45 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years from cure date in cool, dry storage. Discard if rancid smell develops or DOS (orange spots) appear.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oils in a soap formula left unsaponified, providing conditioning free fatty acids in the finished bar.
The point during soap making when the batter has emulsified enough that a drizzle leaves a visible trail on the surface. The batter is ready to pour at light trace.
The 4 to 8 week resting period after unmoulding cold-process soap. During cure, remaining water evaporates and saponification completes, producing a harder, milder bar.
The principal active compound in neem oil, responsible for its well-documented cleansing and skin-supporting properties. It partially survives the saponification process in cold-process soap.
The chemical reaction between an oil or fat and an alkali that produces soap and glycerol.

content.text: Neem oil has a reputation that precedes it, mostly on account of its smell. Fresh from the bottle it is pungent and earthy, somewhere between garlic and sulphur. That scent does not survive a long
at all. By 6 to 8 weeks most of it has gone, leaving a mild, unremarkable bar well suited to oily or congested skin. The active compound
process and contributes to the bar's reputation. This formula keeps neem at 15% to balance efficacy with wearability. Shelf life is 2 years from the cure date.
This recipe uses sodium hydroxide. Read the full
technique before starting. Wear goggles and protective gloves throughout. Always pour sodium hydroxide into water, not the reverse. Work in a ventilated space.

items.prepNote: Confirm with a lye calculator using your exact oils

content.text: technique. Weigh all oils together in a mixing bowl. Melt coconut oil if solid. In a separate jug, carefully add sodium hydroxide to the distilled water and stir until fully dissolved. Cool both to 40 to 45 degrees Celsius.
Pour the lye solution into the oils, stirring as you go. Stick-blend in short bursts until

is reached. Pour into the mould, cover, and insulate for 48 hours. Unmould, cut, and cure on an open rack for at least 4 weeks. A 6 to 8 week cure is strongly recommended to allow the neem scent to mellow fully.
keeps the bar conditioning rather than stripping. This matters most on the face and neck, where neem soap is often used.

Cured bars keep for 2 years in a cool, dry place. Store in paper or on an open rack. Discard if the bar develops a rancid smell or shows orange-brown spots (DOS). Neem has a natural deep yellow-green colour that fades to tan over the cure period.
